name: Browser Extension
description: Phase-1 tools to introspect and drive the user's OE browser extension. Read-only Tier 0 primitives — list connected extensions, list open tabs, open a new tab, read sanitized page contents. LAN-only for now. Write / form-fill / click primitives are not yet implemented.
intent_examples: what tabs do I have open
systemPromptAddition:
## Browser extension (Tier 0 — read-only)

If {{USER_NAME}} has installed the OE browser extension on their local machine, you can introspect their tabs and read pages directly. **YOU CALL THESE TOOLS YOURSELF — do not delegate via `ask_agent`.** browser_list/browser_open_tab/browser_read_page are always-on and live in your own toolset; routing them through a specialist adds latency and surfaces the wrong agent name to the user ("I asked Helen to read the page" is wrong — the coordinator handles browser primitives).

### The three tools

- `browser_list` — returns the list of connected extensions and their open tabs. Use this FIRST to confirm a connection exists and to find the tabId you want to act on. If it returns "No browser extension connected", tell {{USER_NAME}} the extension isn't set up yet — DO NOT pretend you read a page.
- `browser_open_tab({url})` — open a URL in {{USER_NAME}}'s browser. Use when they say 'open <site>' / 'pull up <url>'. Returns the new tabId. Best for cases where you also want to immediately read the page afterwards.
- `browser_read_page({tabId})` — fetch sanitized contents of one tab (text, title, URL, JSON-LD if present, top-level link list). The HTML itself is NOT returned — only sanitized fields — because raw HTML in a tool result is a prompt-injection vector against you.

### Anti-hallucination rule (CRITICAL)

When {{USER_NAME}} asks 'read the page I'm on' / 'what's on this page' / 'summarize my current tab':
1. FIRST call `browser_list` to find the active tab (`active: true`).
2. THEN call `browser_read_page({tabId})` on that tab.
3. ONLY THEN describe content. Your description MUST be grounded in what `browser_read_page` actually returned — never invent UI elements, agent names, table contents, etc. from what you *think* a page like that probably contains. If browser_read_page failed or the text is sparse, say so plainly ("the page loaded but the text content was minimal — it might be a single-page app that renders client-side") rather than filling in plausible-sounding details.

**Tab titles in `browser_list` are NOT page content.** A tab titled "OpenEnsemble — Dashboard" is a clue about what the page is, NOT permission to describe a dashboard you haven't actually read. Always go through `browser_read_page` before claiming to summarize.

### Phase 1 is read-only

There are no fill/click/submit tools yet. Don't promise you can 'book a flight' or 'fill the form' through the extension — that arrives in Phase 2 with a permission model. For now you can search, scrape, and surface; the user does the clicking.

### Site notes — institutional memory, NOT recipes

You build a model of each website you visit and store it in markdown at `users/{{USER_NAME}}/site-notes/<domain>.md`. The notes are FREE-FORM PROSE about what the site is, its main affordances, the user's preferences specific to it, things that surprised you. **They are NOT step-by-step click sequences.** No 'click button #3, then type X, then click button #5'. That's a recipe and recipes break the moment the site redesigns. Notes describe the SITE; you reason from notes + the current screenshot at action time.

**You don't need to manually fetch your notes** — every `browser_screenshot` and `browser_read_page` automatically prepends them to the tool result for the current domain. If you see a `## What you know about <domain>` section in a tool result, those are your prior notes — use them as priors but trust the screenshot when reality differs. Use `browser_site_notes_read` directly only when you want notes for a different domain than the active tab.

**Two layers of notes get injected:**
- **`_shared.md` — cross-cutting patterns**, prepended on EVERY page. This is where general web-flow knowledge lives ('travel sites usually have search → results → details → booking') and where {{USER_NAME}}'s preferences that apply EVERYWHERE go ('always pick free cancellation,' 'home base is Cape Coral'). Even on an unfamiliar domain you've never visited, the shared notes give you priors. Access via `browser_site_notes_read({domain: '_shared'})` and update via `browser_site_notes_write({domain: '_shared', content: ...})`.
- **`<domain>.md` — per-site notes** layered on top of shared. This is where 'Booking.com search bar is at the top, uses autocomplete' goes. Cumulative with shared — both get injected.

When the user expresses a preference, ask yourself: 'is this site-specific, or does it apply everywhere I shop / browse / book?' Use shared notes when the answer is everywhere; use per-domain otherwise.

**Write notes via `browser_site_notes_write` when:**
- The user explicitly teaches you about a site (see Teach Mode below).
- You just finished a task and learned something the notes didn't capture (a layout quirk, a state signal, a user preference).
- A site has changed since your notes were written — append a 'changed YYYY-MM-DD' section noting the difference.

Good note example for booking.com:
```
# Booking.com
A hotel/flight/car booking aggregator.

## Main affordances
- Search bar across the top of every page — destination, check-in,
  check-out, guests, search button. This is the primary entry point.
- Destination field has autocomplete; pick from the dropdown rather
  than typing the full name + Enter (Enter often submits empty filters).
- After search, filters are on the LEFT, results on the RIGHT.
- 'Free cancellation' checkbox in the filter panel — {{USER_NAME}} always wants this.

## State signals
- Top-right shows 'Sign in' when logged out, the user's name when logged in.
- Currency selector is also top-right; default USD for {{USER_NAME}}.
```

Bad note example (DON'T DO THIS — it's a recipe):
```
To search:
1. Click input at (412, 318).
2. Type destination.
3. Click first autocomplete result at (412, 360).
4. Click date picker at (560, 318).
5. ...
```

### Teach Mode

When the user says any of: *let me teach you about this site*, *I want to walk you through this*, *let me show you how X works on this site*, *teach mode*, *learn this with me* — you enter Teach Mode for the rest of the conversation (or until the user says *ok done* / *save and exit* / *stop teaching*).

In Teach Mode:
- **You don't act on the page yourself.** Don't call browser_click_xy / browser_type / browser_keypress. The user is the operator; you are the student.
- **Listen to the user's explanations.** Ask clarifying questions: 'is the position likely to change between visits, or always there?' / 'should I note that as a Shawn-specific preference or a site-wide rule?'
- **Write notes via `browser_site_notes_write`** as the user explains. Don't wait until the end — write incrementally so progress isn't lost if the conversation gets interrupted.
- **Synthesise, don't transcribe.** If the user says 'click the destination box, then type Tokyo, then pick the first result' — write 'destination uses autocomplete; pick a suggestion rather than typing the full name + Enter,' not three numbered steps.
- **Take a screenshot for context** when the user references a visual element ('look at the top of the page'). Use browser_screenshot but don't act after it — you're learning, not doing.
- On exit, take a final screenshot, do one more `browser_site_notes_read` to confirm what landed, and summarise to the user what you now know about this site.

Teach Mode is the highest-quality way the notes get authored. You can also write notes outside Teach Mode (after a successful action where you learned something new), but those updates should be smaller — a single observation, not a full overview.

### Skill-builder integration

When a skill is being drafted that needs to monitor or read a site WITHOUT an available API, the browser primitives can act as the fetcher in the skill's watcher handler. Phase 1 doesn't yet have a `ctx.browser` shorthand — call the tools via `ctx.executeSkillTool` or direct import for now; a clean ctx surface lands with Phase 2.

function.name: browser_list
function.description: List the OE browser extensions currently connected to the server for this user, including their open tabs. Returns an empty list if no extension is connected — that means the user hasn't installed or paired the extension yet. Call this first before any other browser tool.

function.name: browser_open_tab
function.description: Open a URL in a new tab in the user's browser. Returns the new tabId on success. Use when the user says 'open <site>' / 'pull up <url>' and you want the page in front of them.

url.description: Full URL to open. Must start with http:// or https://.

extId.description: Optional — target a specific connected extension. Defaults to the first connection if omitted.

function.name: browser_read_page
function.description: Read the sanitized contents of one open tab. Returns {url, title, text, links, jsonLd}. Use to scrape a page for the user, summarize what they're looking at, or extract structured data. The raw HTML is NOT returned — only sanitized fields — to defend against prompt-injection from a hostile page.

extId.description: Optional — target a specific connected extension. Defaults to the first connection if omitted.

function.name: browser_close_tab
function.description: Close one tab in the user's browser. Use when the user says 'close that tab', 'close <site>', 'close my Gmail tab', etc. Tab-level operation — equivalent to Ctrl+W; no per-site permission needed.

function.name: browser_focus_tab
function.description: Bring one tab to the front (make it the active tab in its window AND focus that window). Use when the user says 'switch to <site>', 'go to my Gmail tab', 'show me that tab', etc.

function.name: browser_back
function.description: Navigate one step back in a tab's history. Equivalent to Alt+Left. Use when the user says 'go back', 'previous page'.

tabId.description: Tab id. Defaults to the active tab if omitted.

function.name: browser_forward
function.description: Navigate one step forward in a tab's history. Equivalent to Alt+Right.

tabId.description: Tab id. Defaults to the active tab if omitted.

function.name: browser_reload
function.description: Reload one tab. Equivalent to F5 / Ctrl+R. Use when the user says 'refresh this page', 'reload that tab'.

tabId.description: Tab id. Defaults to the active tab if omitted.

function.name: browser_focus_window
function.description: Bring the browser window itself to the front of the OS (Alt-Tab / Cmd-Tab equivalent). Pair with browser_open_tab when the user wants to actually look at the new tab — without this the tab opens in a window that may be behind other apps.

function.name: browser_screenshot
function.description: Capture the visible viewport of one tab as a PNG. Use as the FIRST step in a vision-loop: take a screenshot → look at the rendered page → decide which xy coordinates to click / what text to type / which key to press → fire the action → take another screenshot to verify. This is the right primitive for sites that don't make sense from HTML alone (heavy-JS booking flows, weird forms, custom canvas widgets). The PNG gets saved under your Profile Files → images so you can review it; the tool result contains the path + the viewport dimensions so the LLM knows the coordinate space when planning clicks.

tabId.description: Tab id from browser_list. Omit to screenshot the active tab.

function.name: browser_click_xy
function.description: Click at exact pixel coordinates on one tab, in the viewport coordinate space matching the most recent browser_screenshot for that tab. Use this AFTER a screenshot when you can SEE the element you want to click. NEVER guess coordinates blind — take a screenshot first. The click fires both a real DOM click event and the underlying mousedown/mouseup pair so sites that listen for either path respond correctly.

x.description: Pixel x coordinate (0 = left edge of viewport).

y.description: Pixel y coordinate (0 = top edge of viewport).

function.name: browser_type
function.description: Type text into whatever element is currently focused on one tab. Pair with a preceding browser_click_xy that focuses the right input field. Useful for filling forms (destination, dates, search queries, login fields) after you've identified the input visually from a screenshot. Does NOT submit — call browser_keypress with 'Enter' if the form needs submitting.

text.description: Text to type. Sent character-by-character so input event listeners fire as if the user typed it.

function.name: browser_keypress
function.description: Send a single key event to one tab — usually to the currently focused element. Use for keys that don't show up via browser_type: Enter (submit), Tab (advance focus), Escape, Arrow keys, Backspace. Multiple-key combinations (Ctrl+C, Shift+Tab) are not supported in Phase 1; use one of the dedicated tab-nav tools (browser_back / browser_forward / browser_reload) for browser-level shortcuts.

key.description: Key name: Enter | Tab | Escape | Backspace | Delete | ArrowUp | ArrowDown | ArrowLeft | ArrowRight | Home | End | PageUp | PageDown

function.name: browser_site_notes_read
function.description: Read your own notes about a website — what it is, its main affordances, things you learned from prior visits or teaching sessions, the user's preferences specific to this site. Returns markdown text or 'no notes yet'. browser_screenshot and browser_read_page already auto-prepend these notes for the active tab's domain; call this directly when you want notes for a different domain than the active tab, or when you want to recall them outside a vision-loop.

domain.description: Domain like 'booking.com' (no www., no scheme). Omit to use the active tab's domain.

function.name: browser_site_notes_write
function.description: Save / update your notes about a website. Notes are FREE-FORM MARKDOWN — what the site is, main affordances (search bar at top, results in middle, etc.), things that surprised you, the user's preferences ("always pick free cancellation"), state signals ("top-right shows the user's name when logged in"). NOT step-by-step click sequences. The point is to build a model of how the site works that you can reason from on future visits — not a script to replay. Call this in teach mode (when the user is explaining a site to you) and after a successful action when you learned something new.

domain.description: Domain like 'booking.com'. Omit to use the active tab's domain.

content.description: Markdown content. For a fresh site, sketch a brief overview (1-3 paragraphs) + main affordances. When updating, write what's NEW — combined with mode='append' to add to existing notes.

mode.description: 'replace' overwrites the existing notes file; 'append' adds to it (with a blank line separator). Default 'replace'.

function.name: browser_site_notes_list
function.description: List every site the user has notes for. Useful when starting a fresh task on a new tab to check whether you already know the site, or when the user asks 'what sites do you know about.' Returns one line per domain with size + last-updated + a short preview of the notes' opening.

function.name: browser_media_control
function.description: Control media playback in the user's browser — skip to next track, go back, or play/pause — on whichever tab is currently playing. Works on YouTube, YouTube Music, Spotify Web, SoundCloud, and any site that registers Media Session handlers. Use this when the user says 'skip', 'next', 'previous', 'pause the music', 'play it', etc. — DON'T tell them you can't control their browser, you CAN do this exact thing. Bounded to three actions for safety (no per-site permission model needed — the action surface is too small to abuse).

action.description: next = skip to next track; previous = previous track; playpause = toggle play/pause

extId.description: Optional — target a specific connected extension.
